questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and hypertension presents with severe pain in his left foot at night for 2 weeks, relieved by hanging his foot over the edge of the bed. On examination, the left foot is pale, cool, and has a punched-out ulcer on the great toe. Capillary refill is delayed, and the left pedal pulses are absent. The right foot is warm with palpable pulses. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The classic triad of rest pain relieved by dependency, ulcer with absent pulses, and unilateral cool extremity points to critical limb ischaemia. Venous insufficiency would have varicose veins and oedema; neuropathic ulcers are painless; cellulitis shows erythema and warmth; acute embolism is sudden onset and may have a proximal source.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presents with a shallow, painless ulcer on the plantar surface of her right foot. She has peripheral neuropathy. On examination, the foot is warm, but the dorsalis pedis pulse is barely palpable. Ankle-brachial pressure index is 0.45. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Despite painless ulcer (due to neuropathy), the ABPI <0.5 indicates severe ischaemia, making critical limb ischaemia the primary cause. Neuropathic ulcers typically have palpable pulses and normal ABPI.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with a history of smoking and type 2 diabetes presents with a painful, malodorous ulcer on his left heel for 3 weeks. Over the past 2 days, he has developed spreading erythema and swelling around the ulcer. His left foot is cool, and the dorsalis pedis pulse is absent. He is febrile (38.5°C) and tachycardic. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The chronic ulcer, absent pulse, and cool foot indicate underlying critical limb ischaemia, despite the acute infection. Cellulitis and necrotising fasciitis would not explain the chronic ischaemic findings; DVT causes swelling not ulcer; osteomyelitis requires bone exposure or imaging.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old male smoker presents with severe pain in his right foot and second toe, which is cyanotic and cold. He has a history of migratory thrombophlebitis. Pulses in the right foot are absent, but femoral and popliteal pulses are normal. There are no other cardiovascular risk factors. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Young smoker with migratory thrombophlebitis, distal ischaemia, and normal proximal pulses is classic for Buerger's disease. Atherosclerotic CLI usually occurs in older patients with risk factors; embolism would have a cardiac source; Raynaud's is bilateral and reversible.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with known atherosclerosis underwent coronary angiography 3 days ago. He now presents with bilateral painful, dusky, mottled toes and a livedo reticularis pattern on his legs. His pedal pulses are palpable. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Recent arterial instrumentation, bilateral involvement, preserved pulses, and livedo reticularis are typical of cholesterol emboli syndrome. Critical limb ischaemia usually has absent pulses and is often unilateral.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old male smoker presents with a one-month history of rest pain in his left foot. On examination, the foot is pale, capillary refill is delayed, and no pulses are palpable. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of critical limb ischaemia?
questions.explanation: ABPI is the first-line non-invasive test to confirm critical limb ischaemia; a value <0.5 is consistent with the diagnosis. Duplex provides anatomical detail but is not the initial diagnostic test. Toe pressure is reserved for cases with non-compressible arteries.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old patient with diabetes and a chronic foot ulcer has an ABPI of 1.3. Which investigation is most appropriate to diagnose critical limb ischaemia?
questions.explanation: Medial calcinosis in diabetes can falsely elevate ABPI. Toe-brachial pressure index or toe pressure is less affected; a toe-brachial index <0.7 or toe pressure <30 mmHg indicates critical ischaemia. Duplex and other imaging are not the first diagnostic step.

questions.stem: Which of the following ankle-brachial pressure index (ABPI) values is most consistent with critical limb ischaemia?
questions.explanation: Critical limb ischaemia is defined by rest pain, ulceration, or gangrene with an ABPI <0.5. A value of 0.4 is below this threshold. 0.6 indicates moderate ischaemia, while 1.3 suggests non-compressible vessels.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with known peripheral arterial disease (claudication) presents with a two-hour history of severe rest pain in his right foot. The foot is cold and pale, with capillary refill >5 seconds; no motor loss is present. Which investigation should be performed urgently to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In acute-on-chronic ischaemia, duplex ultrasound is quick, non-invasive, and can identify the site of occlusion or stenosis. CT angiography may be used but can delay management; ABPI may be technically difficult due to pain. Duplex is the appropriate first-line confirming test.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old male smoker presents with rest pain in his right foot for 2 weeks. There is no ulcer or gangrene. Ankle-brachial pressure index (ABPI) is 0.3. Duplex ultrasound shows a focal stenosis in the superficial femoral artery with good distal run-off. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend endovascular revascularisation as first-line for CLI with suitable anatomy. The patient has a focal stenosis and good run-off, making angioplasty appropriate. Conservative management is insufficient for rest pain, and surgery is reserved for more extensive disease or failed endovascular treatment.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with critical limb ischaemia (rest pain and a small toe ulcer) has no contraindications to medical therapy. Her cholesterol is 4.5 mmol/L. Which medication is essential for all patients with CLI to reduce cardiovascular risk and improve limb outcomes?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends high-intensity statin therapy (e.g. atorvastatin 80 mg) regardless of baseline cholesterol for all patients with symptomatic peripheral arterial disease, including CLI, to reduce cardiovascular events and improve limb outcomes. Antiplatelets are also indicated but statin is the most essential for risk reduction.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with critical limb ischaemia has a non-healing heel ulcer and rest pain. Angiography shows no suitable distal target for bypass or angioplasty. He is not fit for major surgery. What drug therapy may be considered in this scenario?
questions.explanation: NICE suggests considering intravenous iloprost (a prostacyclin analogue) for patients with CLI who are not suitable for revascularisation. The other vasoactive drugs (naftidrofuryl, pentoxifylline, cilostazol) are not recommended for CLI; they are used in intermittent claudication.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with critical limb ischaemia (gangrenous second toe) and chronic atrial fibrillation on warfarin (INR 2.5) requires revascularisation. Which revascularisation approach is most appropriate given his anticoagulation?
questions.explanation: Endovascular revascularisation carries a lower bleeding risk than surgery and does not require interruption of warfarin. Thrombolysis is contraindicated in established tissue loss and with therapeutic INR. Surgery would require reversal of anticoagulation, increasing thromboembolic risk.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old diabetic patient with known critical limb ischaemia presents with a wet, gangrenous forefoot and spreading cellulitis up to the ankle. There is gas on X-ray. He has a temperature of 38.5°C and a white cell count of 22 × 10⁹/L. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
questions.explanation: Wet gangrene with gas and spreading infection indicates a non-salvageable limb with life-threatening sepsis. Urgent major amputation is required. Below-knee amputation is preferred if the knee joint is viable, as it preserves function and aids rehabilitation. Antibiotics alone or revascularisation will not control the necrotising infection.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with rest pain in his right foot for 3 weeks and a small ulcer on the dorsum of the foot. He has no palpable pedal pulses. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Prescribe oral antibiotics for the ulcer
Start aspirin 75 mg and clopidogrel 75 mg daily
Arrange urgent duplex ultrasound of the leg arteries
Refer urgently to the vascular surgical team
Request urgent ankle-brachial pressure index (ABPI) measurement
questions.explanation: NICE guidance states that patients with suspected critical limb ischaemia (rest pain with tissue loss) should be referred immediately to a vascular service. While ABPI and duplex ultrasound are useful, they should not delay specialist referral.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman with known CLI undergoes angiography revealing a popliteal artery stenosis. Three hours later she develops acute severe pain, a cold, white, pulseless left foot. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Call the vascular consultant for advice
Start IV unfractionated heparin (e.g., 5000 units bolus, then infusion)
Re-examine the foot and document findings
questions.explanation: This is acute limb ischaemia. The first step is anticoagulation with IV heparin to prevent thrombus propagation while arranging urgent surgical or endovascular revascularization. Calling the consultant is important but does not precede giving heparin.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with diabetes presents with a foul-smelling, purulent ulcer on his heel. His foot is warm but has no palpable pulses. He is febrile (38.5°C) and tachycardic. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to vascular surgery for same-day review
Request an urgent MRI of the foot
Debride the ulcer in the bedside treatment room
Apply a non-adherent dressing and review in clinic
Start empirical intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav)
questions.explanation: Given the signs of sepsis (fever, tachycardia) and deep infection, the immediate priority is to start antibiotics. While urgent vascular review is needed, antibiotics should not be delayed.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man undergoes successful femoropopliteal bypass for CLI. On day 2, he develops severe pain in the operated leg. The foot is pale and the Doppler signal over the graft is absent. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Immediately re-explore the bypass graft in theatre
Elevate the leg and apply warm packs
questions.explanation: Acute graft occlusion requires urgent revascularisation. The highest chance of salvage is with immediate return to theatre to re-explore the graft. While heparin may be given en route, the definitive action is surgery.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with CLI undergoes CT angiography with 120 mL of iodinated contrast. Twelve hours later, his urine output is reduced and serum creatinine has risen from 90 to 180 µmol/L. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Repeat the CT angiogram with lower contrast dose
Give IV 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L over 6 hours
Stop all nephrotoxic medications including metformin
questions.explanation: Contrast-induced acute kidney injury (CI-AKI) is treated with IV fluids to maintain renal perfusion. N-acetylcysteine is no longer recommended by NICE. Dialysis is rarely required acutely. The first step is volume expansion.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with known peripheral artery disease presents with a 2-week history of rest pain and now a cold, pulseless left foot. There is a sharp line of colour change at the mid-calf, with the foot pale and mottled. Which finding indicates that the limb is non-viable (irreversible ischaemia)?
questions.explanation: Fixed staining (mottling) indicates irreversible tissue necrosis; loss of motor function can sometimes be reversible if revascularized promptly, whereas fixed staining is a sign of irreversible ischaemia requiring amputation.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman with diabetes and critical limb ischaemia is admitted with a painful, swollen, erythematous right foot. Temperature 38.8°C, HR 110, BP 90/60, RR 22. There is a foul smell and crepitus on palpation. What is the most likely life-threatening complication?
questions.explanation: Crepitus and systemic upset with foul smell indicate gas-forming infection (necrotising fasciitis/gas gangrene), a surgical emergency requiring immediate debridement. Cellulitis does not cause crepitus or systemic toxicity to this degree.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and peripheral neuropathy presents with a non-healing ulcer on his left heel for 3 months. The foot is warm, and both dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are easily palpable. The skin is dry and scaly. He denies any pain. Which feature is most suggestive that this is NOT critical limb ischaemia?
questions.explanation: Critical limb ischaemia is defined by haemodynamic compromise; palpable pulses essentially rule out CLI. The ulcer is likely neuropathic due to diabetes. Absence of rest pain is also supportive, but palpable pulses is the definitive discriminator.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man with a history of smoking and hypertension presents with acute onset of severe pain in his right calf and foot while sitting. On examination, the right foot is pale, cold, and has no palpable pulses below the femoral. There is loss of sensation in the toes. Which finding is most suggestive of an embolic rather than thrombotic occlusion?
options: Symptoms developed over minutes to hours
The contralateral leg also feels cool
questions.explanation: Embolic occlusion typically presents with sudden, severe pain and rapid onset (over minutes to hours) of ischaemia, whereas thrombotic occlusion in a chronically diseased vessel develops more gradually. Atrial fibrillation is a risk factor but not a direct clinical finding.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with known critical limb ischaemia being treated conservatively develops increasing rest pain. The foot becomes swollen and tense, and she is unable to move her toes. Which finding most strongly suggests compartment syndrome?
options: Pain on passive stretch of the toes
questions.explanation: Pain on passive stretch of the toes is the classic early sign of compartment syndrome. Loss of pulse is a late sign. Numbness may also occur but is less specific. In CLI, muscle infarction can mimic compartment syndrome, but passive stretch pain is key.
